lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market wholesale and mixed-channel growers of nursery stock, ornamental shrubs and trees, fruit and nut plants, Christmas trees, sod, and short-rotation woody crops, emphasizing propagation, contract growing, broad inventories, climate-suitable varieties, delivery coordination, and repeat landscaper, retailer, municipal, and institutional accounts.
lens.excluded_activities: Retail garden centers primarily reselling purchased plants; landscaping and installation services; forest nurseries and timber tracts with longer rotations; floriculture without nursery or tree production; captive municipal or institutional nurseries; hobby growers; and businesses outside the LMM earnings band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include landscape contractors, garden centers, home-improvement retailers, wholesalers, municipalities, institutions, orchards, forestry programs, and direct consumers. Revenue is earned per plant, tree, pallet, acreage unit, or crop lot; the service-like subset combines forward growing plans, reserved inventory, propagation, specifications, tagging, staged releases, logistics, and repeat seasonal replenishment.

a.bridge: O*NET nursery and crop-labor tasks are translated into total nursery labor exposure, while USDA nursery-specific surveys document automation opportunities in irrigation, plant handling, transport, scouting, spraying, inventory, pruning, and labeling. The evidence is directly sector-relevant but mostly concerns mechanization and automation rather than measured generative-AI labor savings.
a.rationale: AI and robotics can assist propagation planning, demand forecasting, inventory recognition, irrigation and fertigation, pest and disease scouting, spraying, spacing and movement, grading, labeling, order allocation, routing, maintenance, customer service, and documentation. Planting, potting, pruning, staking, digging, loading, transplanting, culling, repairs, and handling irregular living plants remain physical and dexterity-intensive.
a.change_evidence: Representative task-time and deployment studies across container and field nurseries measuring labor, inventory accuracy, water and chemicals, shrink, quality, throughput, order errors, maintenance, and administrative hours attributable to AI or automation.
caveats: O*NET combines crop, nursery, and greenhouse labor.
Surveyed automation is not synonymous with generative AI.
Container, field, tree, sod, and Christmas-tree operations have different task mixes.

rho.rationale: Timers, sensors, mobile equipment, RFID, cameras, drones, and nursery-management systems provide an adoption base. Realization is constrained by irregular plants and layouts, outdoor weather, multi-year crop cycles, inconsistent labels and inventory, seasonal labor, integration cost, limited technical staff, fragmented software, water systems, uncertain ROI, and lower adoption among smaller and field nurseries. USDA reports high perceived irrigation benefits but plateauing adoption.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after evidence by nursery type and size showing implementation cost, adoption, irrigation and handling automation, labor, water, chemicals, shrink, turns, inventory accuracy, quality, uptime, and payback.
caveats: Current adoption is materially higher in larger and container nurseries.
Unstructured outdoor work remains harder than fixed greenhouse automation.
Long crop cycles delay feedback and return measurement.

e.bridge: USDA's 2024 horticulture census shows wholesale as the dominant channel and landscape contractors as a major channel, while cross-crop USDA evidence explains contracts specifying price, quantity, quality, and delivery and compensating differentiated attributes. These mechanisms are mapped to the share of nurseries with repeat, planned, service-like customer programs.
e.rationale: Large landscapers, retailers, municipalities, and orchard customers often plan varieties, sizes, volumes, tags, timing, and delivery months in advance, enabling repeat relationships and contract growing. Spot wholesale, auction, Christmas-tree, sod, and direct-retail sales remain transactional; biological growth and inventory risk usually stays with the grower.
e.change_evidence: Nursery-level revenue by contract growing, reserved inventory, standing wholesale accounts, replenishment programs, municipal bids, direct retail, and spot sales, with repeat-order, retention, and forecast-commitment data.
caveats: Wholesale sales are not automatically recurring contracts.
General crop-contract evidence is not nursery-specific.
Customer forecasts may be cancellable and leave the grower with living inventory.

s5.bridge: Gallup's cross-industry five-year owner-disposition survey is mapped to actionable availability among qualifying nurseries. It may overstate supply because land and water rights, seasonal labor, crop inventory, phytosanitary compliance, customer concentration, owner horticultural knowledge, equipment, and working capital complicate transfer.
s5.rationale: The frozen dataset supplies no defensible firm count. A transferable LMM nursery needs controlled land and water, healthy and accurately valued living inventory, propagation and growing expertise, labor continuity, irrigation, equipment, clean pest history, logistics, diverse repeat customers, and sufficient forward production. Many small nurseries are owner-dependent or property-led transactions.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year nursery transactions, succession, closures, and failed sales, including revenue, inventory age and salability, owner age, land tenure, water, labor, crop health, customer retention, debt, and buyer type.
caveats: The dataset intentionally provides no n-band estimate.
Cross-industry owner intentions are not observed nursery transactions.
Living inventory and real estate can dominate enterprise value.

q.bridge: USDA documents concentrated wholesale channels, strong recent nursery-stock sales, and product categories with multi-year growing requirements; crop-contract evidence supports specified quality and delivery. These are mapped to AI-value retention through availability, quality, assortment, and fulfillment, while large retailers and landscapers can exert strong purchasing leverage.
q.rationale: Correct varieties and sizes, healthy stock, long lead times, local climate adaptation, phytosanitary status, broad assortment, reliable fills, and delivery timing create switching friction and permit retention of lower shrink and better planning. Standard plants, transparent wholesale lists, retailer scale, substitutes, brokered supply, and seasonal discounting force pass-through.
q.change_evidence: Account-level pricing and margins showing reservations, deposits, contract growing, substitutions, fill rates, shrink, delivery fees, markdowns, retailer chargebacks, customer churn, and post-automation price retention.
caveats: Capture is higher for scarce mature specimens and proprietary or local varieties.
Large buyers can shift volume or demand annual concessions.
Weather-driven shortages can temporarily exaggerate pricing power.

d5.bridge: USDA NASS reports 2024 nursery-stock sales of $5.34 billion, up 17% from 2019, with gains across major tree and shrub categories. This five-year nominal industry observation is translated cautiously into a forward real-demand range, recognizing inflation, the broader horticulture scope, housing and landscaping cycles, and uneven categories.
d5.rationale: Residential and commercial landscaping, replacement plantings, municipal canopy and resilience programs, orchard renewal, storm recovery, outdoor living, and water-efficient or climate-adapted landscapes support demand. Housing cycles, interest rates, construction, discretionary spending, drought and water rules, extreme weather, imports, labor, and retailer inventory corrections create downside.
d5.change_evidence: Independent 2026-2031 real unit and price forecasts for U.S. nursery stock, ornamental trees and shrubs, fruit and nut plants, Christmas trees, sod, and short-rotation trees by customer channel and region.
caveats: The observed 17% growth is nominal, not real.
Horticulture census data aggregate multiple business models and sizes.
Recent category growth may not persist through a housing downturn.

o.rationale: AI cannot replace living plants, trees, shade, landscaping, habitat, or biological propagation and is more likely to improve production and selection. Synthetic décor, virtual design, reduced landscaping area, or optimized planting density may affect selected demand, but physical nursery stock remains durable over five years.
o.change_evidence: Commercial evidence that AI-driven design, synthetic landscaping, or other substitutions materially reduce physical nursery and tree purchases rather than change assortment or improve planning.
caveats: The factor isolates AI-related obsolescence from housing and landscaping cycles.
Some ornamental categories are more discretionary than trees for restoration or food production.
Climate shifts can obsolete specific varieties without eliminating the industry.

narrative.executive_view: Nursery and tree production is labor-intensive, inventory-heavy, and increasingly suited to irrigation, vision, inventory, and planning automation. The best LMM target is a scaled wholesale nursery with water security, accurate living inventory, broad climate-suitable varieties, and repeat landscaper, retailer, municipal, or orchard programs rather than a small owner-dependent garden center.
narrative.ai_impact: Demand forecasting, propagation schedules, irrigation, scouting, spraying, plant counting, grading, labeling, movement, order allocation, routing, and administration are attractive. Irregular living products still require substantial hands-on planting, pruning, spacing, digging, loading, and culling.
narrative.value_capture: Long growing cycles, scarce sizes and varieties, local adaptation, quality, broad assortment, fill rates, and delivery reliability support retention. Standard plants and concentrated large buyers reduce it.
narrative.firm_availability: There is no defensible frozen firm count. Transferability turns on land and water, inventory health and valuation, propagation know-how, pest status, labor, irrigation, equipment, customer concentration, and forward production. Real estate and living inventory can complicate conventional acquisition structures.
narrative.demand_durability: USDA reports broad five-year nominal gains in nursery stock, supported by landscaping, replacement, urban canopy, resilience, orchard, and outdoor-living demand. Housing, construction, water restrictions, interest rates, weather, and retailer destocking remain significant cyclical risks.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key uncertainties are missing labor-share and firm-count data, crop and channel mix, living-inventory valuation, shrink, water, labor, pest and disease exposure, housing sensitivity, land tenure, seasonality, customer concentration, logistics, and the true ROI of automation in field nurseries.
narrative.principal_driver: Long-cycle living inventory and labor shortages make better forecasting, irrigation, handling, counting, and order allocation especially valuable.
narrative.principal_weakness: Biological variability, working-capital intensity, land and water dependence, and hands-on plant handling limit automation and complicate acquisition diligence.

sources.title: NAICS 111421 - Nursery and Tree Production
sources.supports: Industry boundary and examples covering open-field and covered nursery products, ornamental shrubs and trees, Christmas trees, tree nurseries, and short-rotation woody crops, with retail and long-cycle forestry excluded.

sources.title: Farmworkers and Laborers, Crop, Nursery, and Greenhouse
sources.supports: Direct labor-task evidence for planting, watering, fertilizing, spraying, weeding, pruning, transplanting, grading, sorting, packing, loading, irrigation, and manual tools.

sources.title: Automated Irrigation Adoption in U.S. Nurseries
sources.supports: Nursery-specific evidence on automated irrigation, labor-saving benefits, adoption levels and plateauing, differences by nursery size and container versus field production, and implementation barriers.

sources.title: U.S. Horticulture Operations Report $18.3 Billion in Sales
sources.supports: 2024 horticulture sales, nursery-stock sales and five-year change, ownership, labor-cost share, and category-level market evidence.

sources.title: Marketing and Production Contracts Are Widely Used in U.S. Agriculture
sources.supports: Cross-crop contract mechanisms covering prices, quantities, quality, delivery, differentiated attributes, assured outlets, production guidance, and contract fees.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Cross-industry owner-age and five-year sale, transfer, closure, and succession-intention evidence.
